title: Quiz: Amplitude Encoding
description: Test your understanding of amplitude encoding concepts

questions.question: How many qubits are needed to amplitude-encode a vector of 1000 values?
questions.explanation: Amplitude encoding uses log₂(N) qubits. For 1000 values, we need ⌈log₂(1000)⌉ = 10 qubits (2¹⁰ = 1024 ≥ 1000).

questions.question: What is the main limitation of amplitude encoding?

options.text: General state preparation requires O(N) gates
It only works on fault-tolerant computers

questions.explanation: While amplitude encoding uses few qubits, preparing an arbitrary amplitude-encoded state generally requires O(N) quantum gates, which can negate the qubit savings.

questions.question: The vector encoded via amplitude encoding must be normalized to have unit length.
questions.explanation: True. Quantum states must have unit norm (|ψ|² = 1), so the classical vector must be normalized before encoding as amplitudes.

questions.question: What theoretical device would enable efficient amplitude encoding?
questions.explanation: QRAM would allow O(log N) query complexity for data access. However, practical QRAM doesn't exist yet, making efficient amplitude encoding challenging.

questions.question: For the vector [3, 4], what is the amplitude-encoded state?
questions.explanation: The vector [3,4] has norm 5 (3-4-5 triangle). Normalized: [3/5, 4/5] = [0.6, 0.8], giving state 0.6|0⟩ + 0.8|1⟩.
